title: What is Image Retrieval?
concept: Image retrieval finds images similar to a query image from a large database — the technology behind 'search by photo' features, using learned visual similarity rather than keyword matching.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Which IDF1 vs MOTA distinction matters most for a system computing individual car trajectories?

options.text: IDF1, since trajectory computation depends heavily on correct identity continuity
Only frame rate matters for trajectories

exercises.explain: Yes! Identity-sensitive tasks benefit most from strong IDF1.

cards.title: Search by photo, not by words
cards.body: You snap a photo of a chair you like in a furniture store and want to find similar chairs online. There are no keywords to type — you need the search engine to understand visual similarity directly from the image itself.

cards.title: Image Retrieval
cards.body: Image retrieval (also called reverse image search or similarity search) finds images in a large database that are visually similar to a given query image, ranked by similarity, without relying on text labels or keywords.

cards.title: A librarian who recognizes book covers
cards.body: A text search librarian finds books by matching keywords in a title. An image retrieval librarian instead looks at a book's COVER art and finds every other book in the library with a visually similar cover — no reading required, just visual matching.

cards.title: Fashion shopping apps
cards.body: A shopping app lets a user photograph an outfit they saw on the street. The app's image retrieval system searches its product catalog for visually similar clothing items, returning ranked matches the user can actually buy.

cards.title: It's not the same as classification
cards.body: It's easy to confuse retrieval with classification. Classification answers 'what category is this?' (e.g. 'dress'). Retrieval answers a different question: 'which SPECIFIC images in my database look most like this one?' — it doesn't need predefined categories at all.

cards.title: Classification vs. Retrieval
cards.body: Two different questions a vision system can answer:

left.text:
Answers: 'What category is this?'
Fixed set of predefined labels
One label output

right.text:
Answers: 'What looks similar to this?'
No predefined categories needed
Ranked list of similar images

exercises.prompt: What question does image retrieval answer?

options.text: Which images in a database look most similar to this query image?
What is the exact object category of this image?
What is the file size of this image?
How many objects are detected in this image?

exercises.explain: Correct! Retrieval finds visually similar images, ranked by similarity.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Image retrieval requires a fixed, predefined set of category labels to work.
exercises.explain: False. Retrieval doesn't need predefined categories — it directly compares visual similarity between images.

exercises.sentence: Image retrieval is also commonly called reverse image ___.
exercises.explain: Reverse image search is another common name for image retrieval.

exercises.prompt: Why is image retrieval a fundamentally different task from image classification?

options.text: Retrieval ranks similar images from a database without needing predefined category labels, unlike classification
Retrieval only works on grayscale images
Classification requires no training data at all

exercises.explain: Correct! Retrieval's open-ended similarity search differs from classification's fixed-label prediction.

exercises.prompt: An e-commerce company wants users to photograph any item and instantly find visually similar products in their catalog, even for brand-new products added yesterday with no training data. Which approach fits best?

options.text: Image retrieval, since it compares visual similarity directly without needing to retrain a classifier for every new product category
Image classification, since it requires zero setup for new categories
Object detection, since it only draws bounding boxes and cannot compare similarity
None of these approaches can handle newly added products

exercises.explain: Exactly! Retrieval's category-free similarity search handles new products instantly. Next: how images get converted into comparable search keys — embeddings.
